summary: Five Kenney Blaster Kit weapons remain registered, but the starting mobile cycle now exposes only SPARK and BORE with distinct stats, colors, mesh projectile shapes, cache-busted GLBs, and ElevenLabs SFX profiles.
observations: SPARK is a fast sidearm using blaster-a.glb.
BORE is a close scatter weapon using blaster-h.glb.
VAULT is a heavy pulse weapon using blaster-p.glb.
Each weapon has an ElevenLabs-generated firing SFX asset mapped by soundProfile.
All three viewmodels use Y rotation 0 after the user requested the guns face the other way.
Shots raycast against the tile level and report lastShot wall hit details through debug state.
Weapon preview/model paths use runtime assetBuild cache invalidation through the manifest helper.
SPARK, BORE, and VAULT view offsets moved to x 0.52, 0.56, and 0.60.
Weapon wall clearance and debug footprint checks are derived from WEAPON_DEFINITIONS through weaponClearance.ts.
WeaponSystem retracts and lowers the viewmodel when the forward ray or weapon probe nears a wall.
Shot tracer origin now uses the active weapon right-side offset instead of a near-center hardcoded start.
Added weaponViewPose.ts so muzzle flash, tracer start, tracer endpoint, and wall impact positions are derived from one camera-local pose helper.
The shared pose helper accounts for weapon view offset, scale, rotation, recoil, and wall-avoidance retraction.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.effectPose exposes rounded shot-effect positions for QA automation.
SPARK keeps its previous first-person framing while BORE and VAULT were lowered for their different silhouettes.
Each weapon now declares view.muzzleLocalOffset in the manifest.
weaponShotMath.ts converts flat X/Z wall raycast distances to camera-space tracer distances so pitch up/down shots still terminate at the reticle.
The visible muzzle flash now sits in shotFeedbackRoot and uses the same camera-local muzzle position as the tracer start.
WeaponAudio now plays committed cache-busted MP3 SFX assets rather than procedural oscillator/noise bursts.
SPARK, BORE, and VAULT muzzle anchors were retuned closer to the visible GLB muzzle fronts.
The visible muzzle flash is now a centered camera-facing CircleGeometry disk attached to shotFeedbackRoot.
BORE and VAULT muzzle anchors were moved up and left relative to their models after visual review.
SPARK, BORE, and VAULT now carry per-weapon muzzle flash, tracer, and wall-impact colors plus scale/timing values in the manifest.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.effectStyle exposes the active effect colors so smoke QA can validate them without timing a one-frame flash screenshot.
The bottom weapon preview tray was removed; weapon switching now happens through [data-weapon-cycle-button].
Latest weapon-cycle and flash screenshots are stored under artifacts/sub-agents/20260704-weapon-cycle-burst/smoke-qa-agent/.
Added RIFT from blaster-q.glb as a slow high-damage precision weapon with green effects.
Added TORCH from blaster-m.glb as a fast low-damage burst weapon with red-pink effects.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.activeWeaponStats exposes damage, fireIntervalMs, reloadMs, and rangeUnits.
The runtime starting roster is now SPARK and BORE only; VAULT, RIFT, and TORCH remain defined assets for future unlock/progression work.
Boot weapon model preloading now requests only SPARK and BORE, reducing starting weapon model payload while preserving the full manifest.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.weaponIds now reports the currently available starting weapons, not every registered weapon definition.
SPARK now uses a pooled cyan cylinder bolt projectile so it reads as a fast sidearm instead of a thin WebGL line.
BORE now uses a pooled orange sphere projectile so its slower, heavier cadence reads differently from SPARK.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.effectStyle.projectile exposes shape, radius, length, opacity, and travel ratio for deterministic smoke QA.
decisions: Expose SPARK and BORE through the starting mobile weapon cycle button.
Use one fire button for this slice.
Leave upgrades, reload button, enemy/projectile behavior, and final hit impact assets for later iterations.
Use primitive tracer and wall impact only as temporary gameplay feedback.
Keep yaw 0 until camera/game-feel review proves a better first-person framing.
Keep viewmodel wall clearance tied to manifest view offsets to avoid future drift.
Route future muzzle flash, tracer, projectile, and impact placement through getWeaponShotEffectPositions.
Keep shape-specific muzzle tuning in each WeaponDefinition.view.muzzleLocalOffset.
Use a centered placeholder flash disk until an external muzzle-flash VFX asset is sourced.
Keep the per-weapon effect style in WeaponDefinition.effects so later external VFX replacement has a clear style target per weapon.
Keep the starting mobile cycle order SPARK, BORE until progression or pickups unlock later weapons.
Use activeWeaponStats as the deterministic QA surface for weapon balance differences.
Keep VAULT, RIFT, and TORCH in WEAPON_DEFINITIONS so future progression can opt them into availability without recreating asset records.
Use pooled mesh projectile primitives for MVP readability because mobile WebGL line width is unreliable across devices.
caughtIssues: Current assets are prop-scale GLB models, not dedicated animated first-person weapon rigs.
Wall hit feedback currently uses code primitives rather than external assets.
Wall avoidance uses a simplified probe and ray rather than final mesh collision.
Current muzzle flash, tracer, and wall impact visuals are procedural placeholders until external VFX assets are sourced.
The primitive tracer is short-lived and can be hard to inspect in still screenshots; prefer effectPose assertions for automated alignment.
The centered colored disk improves flash alignment but is still a procedural placeholder.
Procedural color/style variants make the registered guns easier to distinguish, but final muzzle/tracer/impact art still needs external assets.
Future unlock code needs an explicit available-weapon inventory rather than reading every WEAPON_DEFINITIONS entry as currently usable.
Projectile meshes are still procedural placeholders; final projectile VFX should come from external assets or particle textures.
risks: Model offsets, right-shifted placement, and yaw 0 presentation may need per-device adjustment once gameplay camera tuning starts.
RIFT and TORCH viewmodel framing still needs screenshot QA on real mobile hardware.
